title: The effect of natural antimicrobials on the Campylobacter coli T6SS<sup>+/-</sup> during in vitro infection assays and on their ability to adhere to chicken skin and carcasses.
author_string: Balta, Igori, Linton, Mark, Pinkerton, Laurette, Kelly, Carmel, Ward, Patrick et al.
abstract: Reducing the Campylobacter load on poultry carcasses represents a major tasks for the industry as its ability to reduce their presence is of major interest aiming to increase consumer safety. This study investigated the ability of a mixture of natural antimicrobials (A3001) to reduce the adherence of the T6SS<sup>+/-</sup>C. coli isolates (NC1<sup>hcp-</sup>, NC2 <sup>hcp-</sup> and NC3 <sup>hcp+</sup>) to chicken neck skin and whole carcasses. Overall, the antimicrobial mixture induced a significant reduction in the capability of our C. coli isolates to colonise the chicken skin (p < 0.05) and carcasses (p < 0.0001) but with a greater effect (≈3 log reduction) on the NC3 isolate. Using the HCT-8 in vitro infection model we also show that at a concentration of 0.5% A3001, the impact on the NC3 isolate is accompanied by the downregulation of the hcp gene (p = 0.0001), and indicator of the T6SS presence. The results described herein also indicated that these isolates are highly resistant to H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, up to 20 mM, suggesting a high resilience to environmental stresses. In summary our study shows that natural antimicrobials can reduce the ability of T6SS positive chicken C. coli isolates to adhere to chicken skin or to the whole carcass and to infect epithelial cells in vitro and could be considered a potential intervention at processor level.
